compiled_state.author: Joe Oakhart

comments.author: Joe Oakhart
comments.body: [G4 container review — parent 4fcd] Scope-overlap flag with sibling 22bf-6af2-a9e8-4e3d. This child (a36b, alias worm-folly-barge) is a bug ticket whose ACs prescribe the same remedy that sibling story 22bf already owns: scope claim freshness to the review's resolved edit-set/cited files instead of whole-HEAD, so an unrelated commit no longer stales the attestation, while a relevant change still invalidates. 22bf's description and ACs explicitly name 'fixes `worm-folly-barge`' and re-implement a36b's AC1/AC2/AC3 verbatim (unrelated-file commit → claim succeeds; dependency-file commit → claim blocked; same attest.py claim_gate_check change). Two sibling tickets carrying overlapping ACs over the same file/behavior risk duplicate or conflicting implementation. Suggested fix: make a36b a non-implementing bug/report that 22bf 'fixes' (or fold a36b's repro/AC into 22bf as a verification scenario), so the scoped-freshness behavior is specified and tested in exactly one place.

comments.author: Joe Oakhart
comments.body:
[G3 container review — parent 4fcd, child a36b] Child coverage: CLEAN, no findings.

4-bucket audit of parent 4fcd's acceptance/success criteria against THIS child (with sibling cross-check across the full roster: 22bf, 5a6c, a36b):

- Parent SC1 / AC1 ("a mechanism DETECTS material drift in the code the review was grounded against — resolved edit-set / cited files"): w.r.t. THIS child = PARTIALLY. a36b is the over-invalidation (false-positive) facet of the same root cause: its AC1 scopes freshness to "the resolved edit-set / cited files rather than the whole HEAD sha" and AC2 requires that "a relevant change (to the cited/edit-set files) STILL invalidates" — i.e. it exercises/requires the same grounded-code drift mechanism. FULL coverage is provided by sibling 22bf (AC: "binds a SIGNED {path: content-hash} map of the dependency set (file_impact ∪ review-cited files); whole-HEAD exact-match staleness check is removed"). No gap.

- Parent AC2 ("drift invalidates/flags the attestation, prompting re-review; cheap enough not to tax hot paths"): w.r.t. THIS child = PARTIALLY. a36b's AC2 (relevant change still invalidates → no regression of the under-invalidation/re-review goal) and AC4 (friction reduced; no full re-review for an unrelated commit) bear on the invalidate→re-review + cost dimension. FULLY covered by sibling 22bf ("cheap — sub-ms for a handful of files; no claim hot-path regression") and refined by 5a6c (narrowed re-review). No gap.

- Parent AC3 ("tested: code drift after a signed review → attestation no longer valid"): w.r.t. THIS child = PARTIALLY. a36b's test AC ("review at X, commit a cited file, claim is blocked") is exactly the parent AC3 scenario, plus the inverse FP test (commit an unrelated file → claim succeeds). FULLY covered by sibling 22bf's test AC (commit a dependency file → claim blocked; delete → blocked; empty-set → any commit blocks). No gap.

Coverage map (parent criterion → covering sibling):
- SC1/AC1 → 22bf (full, content-hash drift detection); a36b (partial — exercises grounded-code scoping via the FP facet); 5a6c structural (Story-2 consumer).
- AC2 → 22bf (full, invalidate + cheapness); a36b (partial — still-invalidate + friction); 5a6c (remedy refinement).
- AC3 → 22bf (full); a36b (partial — cited-file-blocked + unrelated-succeeds tests); 5a6c (narrowed-path tests).

No parent acceptance/success criterion is left uncovered by the union of children, and this child introduces no coverage hole. Empty findings for G3 on this pairing.

comments.author: Joe Oakhart
comments.body:
[G4 container review — parent 4fcd, child a36b `worm-folly-barge` | sibling cross-check: 22bf, 5a6c] One finding (mode: scope overlap). The other 6 cross-child modes read clean (details below).

FINDING — Scope overlap (this child a36b ⟷ sibling 22bf `jerky-vista-amok`)
- finding: a36b's prescriptive ACs re-specify the SAME scoped-freshness remedy that sibling story 22bf already owns and explicitly claims to fix. Two independently-open sibling tickets carry overlapping ACs over the identical file + behavior, with ambiguous ownership of the implementation and its tests.
- criteria: parent AC1 ("a mechanism detects material drift in the code the review was grounded against — e.g. the resolved edit-set / cited files"); parent AC3 ("tested: code drift after a signed review → attestation no longer valid").
- location: `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py` `claim_gate_check` step-3 (whole-HEAD exact-match staleness check); this is both the subject of bug a36b and the line 22bf replaces.
- evidence:
  - a36b ACs prescribe the remedy: AC1 "Claim freshness is scoped to the code the review was grounded against (resolved edit-set / cited files) rather than the whole HEAD sha, so an unrelated commit does NOT stale the attestation"; AC2 "A relevant change (to the cited/edit-set files) STILL invalidates"; test AC "review at X, commit an unrelated file, claim succeeds; review at X, commit a cited file, claim is blocked."
  - Sibling 22bf names the SAME fix and target: description "Content-addressing makes this … immune to … shared-checkout HEAD moves — the specific `worm-folly-barge` complaint"; AC2 "Claim SUCCEEDS when an unrelated file changed after signing (fixes `worm-folly-barge`); claim is BLOCKED when a dependency file's content changed"; test AC "review at X, commit an unrelated file → claim succeeds; commit a dependency file → claim blocked."
  - Both target the identical `claim_gate_check` step-3 replacement and carry near-verbatim, overlapping test scenarios (unrelated-file → succeeds; dependency/cited-file → blocked). a36b is currently an independently-open BUG ticket (status: open) with prescriptive implementation ACs, not a folded-in report.
  - The relationship edge is loose: a36b `relates_to` 22bf (not `fixes`/blocking), so neither ticket pins single ownership of the change.
- scenarios:
  (a) Both 22bf and a36b are independently claimed/implemented → two competing edits to `claim_gate_check` step-3 and duplicate/conflicting test suites for the same unrelated-file-succeeds / cited-file-blocked behavior.
  (b) a36b is implemented as a narrower whole-HEAD tweak that diverges from 22bf's content-addressed `{path: content-hash}` design → two inconsistent fixes for one root cause.
- impact: Risk of duplicate or conflicting implementation of one behavior across two open sibling tickets; ambiguous ownership of the `worm-folly-barge` fix and its tests.
- checklist_item: scope overlap.
- suggested_fix: Make exactly one ticket own the scoped-freshness implementation + tests. Either (i) convert a36b into a non-implementing bug/report that 22bf "fixes" (fold its repro + unrelated-file/cited-file scenarios into 22bf's test AC as verification), or (ii) upgrade the a36b→22bf link to an explicit `fixes`/blocking relation and strip a36b's prescriptive implementation ACs, leaving it as the reproduction/acceptance witness. Either way the `claim_gate_check` step-3 change and its scenarios live in one place. (This overlap was also recorded from 22bf's side; noting it here so the duplication is visible from both tickets.)

The other 6 modes (high-confidence, benign-reading filter applied):
- Implicit shared state — CLEAN for a36b: the signed-manifest-schema coordination concern lives on the 22bf⟷5a6c axis (both extend `build_manifest`/`sign_plan_review`'s signed payload). a36b is a bug report and prescribes no manifest-schema write of its own. Not flagged.
- Conflicting assumptions — CLEAN: a36b's diagnosis ("the code-binding is too coarse (whole-HEAD) rather than scoped to the review's resolved edit-set / cited files") agrees with the parent and with 22bf/5a6c's dependency basis (`file_impact` ∪ citations). No contradiction.
- Dependency gap — CLEAN: a36b declares `relates_to` 22bf; the fix-relevant edge between the bug and its remedy story is present (its tightness is addressed in the scope-overlap fix above, not a missing-edge gap).
- Ordering violation — CLEAN: a36b is a bug/witness, not a story sequenced ahead of or behind another deliverable; no ordering precedence is created or violated. (The Story 1 → Story 2 ordering is the 22bf → 5a6c axis, correctly carried by 5a6c `depends_on` 22bf.)
- Consumer impact — CLEAN: the sole claim-path consumer (`src/rebar/_commands/claim.py`) reads `ok`/`reason`; the scoped-freshness fix changes the `stale-head` verdict internals only, not the gate's read contract. No dangling consumer introduced by a36b.
- Residual references — CLEAN: a36b introduces no orphaned/duplicate reference; it points at the existing `stale-head` behavior and the `require_plan_review_for_claim` gate, both real.

comments.author: Joe Oakhart
comments.body:
[G4 container review — parent 4fcd, child a36b `worm-folly-barge` | sibling cross-check: 22bf, 5a6c] One finding (mode: scope overlap). The other 6 cross-child modes read clean.

FINDING — Scope overlap (this child a36b ⟷ sibling 22bf `jerky-vista-amok`)
- criteria: parent AC1 ("a mechanism detects material drift in the code the review was grounded against — e.g. the resolved edit-set / cited files"); parent AC3 ("tested: code drift after a signed review → attestation no longer valid").
- location: `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py` `claim_gate_check` step-3 (whole-HEAD exact-match staleness check). a36b and 22bf both target this exact code site with the same remedy and overlapping test scenarios.
- evidence:
  - a36b prescribes implementation ACs: AC1 "Claim freshness is scoped to the code the review was grounded against (resolved edit-set / cited files) rather than the whole HEAD sha, so an unrelated commit does NOT stale the attestation"; AC2 "A relevant change (to the cited/edit-set files) STILL invalidates"; test AC "review at X, commit an unrelated file, claim succeeds; review at X, commit a cited file, claim is blocked."
  - Sibling 22bf prescribes the SAME remedy and explicitly claims to fix this ticket: description "Content-addressing makes this … immune to … shared-checkout HEAD moves — the specific `worm-folly-barge` complaint"; AC2 "Claim SUCCEEDS when an unrelated file changed after signing (fixes `worm-folly-barge`); claim is BLOCKED when a dependency file's content changed or was deleted"; test AC "review at X, commit an unrelated file → claim succeeds; commit a dependency file → claim blocked."
  - Both edit `claim_gate_check` step-3 and carry near-verbatim overlapping test scenarios (unrelated-file → succeeds; cited/dependency-file → blocked). a36b is currently independently open (status: open) with prescriptive implementation ACs, not a closed reproduction folded into 22bf. The two are linked only by a non-directional `relates_to` edge, leaving ownership of the fix + tests ambiguous.
  - a36b's own context paragraph already concedes the shared root cause and that "The mechanism in this epic's AC1 … would fix both facets at once," reinforcing that the implementing work belongs in one ticket (22bf), not duplicated here.
- scenarios: (a) Both a36b and 22bf get independently claimed/implemented → two competing edits to `claim_gate_check` step-3 and duplicate/conflicting test suites for the same unrelated-file-succeeds / cited-file-blocked behavior. (b) a36b is implemented as a narrower whole-HEAD tweak that diverges from 22bf's content-addressed `{path: content-hash}` design → inconsistent fixes for one root cause.
- impact: Duplicate or conflicting implementation of one behavior across two open sibling tickets; ambiguous ownership of the `worm-folly-barge` fix and its tests; risk of regressing 22bf's under-invalidation goal if a36b is fixed in isolation.
- checklist_item: scope overlap.
- suggested_fix: Make exactly one ticket own the scoped-freshness implementation + tests. Either (i) convert a36b into a non-implementing bug/acceptance witness that 22bf "fixes" (fold its repro + unrelated-file/cited-file scenarios into 22bf's test AC), or (ii) replace the `relates_to` edge with a directional `fixes`/blocking link a36b→22bf and strip a36b's prescriptive implementation ACs. Either way the `claim_gate_check` step-3 change and its test scenarios live in one place (22bf).

The other 6 modes (high-confidence, benign-reading filter applied):
- Implicit shared state — CLEAN w.r.t. a36b: a36b is a bug report and does not itself write into the signed `attest.py` manifest; the manifest-schema sharing concern is between 22bf and 5a6c, not this child.
- Conflicting assumptions — CLEAN: a36b's premise (binding is whole-HEAD, too coarse) matches 22bf's premise and the live code (`claim_gate_check` step-3 exact-match; `material_fingerprint` binds declared `file_impact`, not on-disk content). a36b's AC2 explicitly preserves the under-invalidation goal ("relevant change STILL invalidates"), consistent with the epic. No contradiction.
- Dependency gap — CLEAN-ish: a36b declares `relates_to` 22bf, so an edge exists; its weakness (non-directional) is captured under the scope-overlap finding above rather than as a separate dependency-gap finding.
- Ordering violation — CLEAN: no sequencing constraint between a36b (the reported symptom) and the stories; 22bf (Story 1) carries the fix, 5a6c (Story 2) is unrelated to a36b.
- Consumer impact — CLEAN: a36b prescribes no change to the gate's read contract; the sole consumer (`src/rebar/_commands/claim.py`) reads `ok`/`reason`, unaffected by a verdict-internal scoping change.
- Residual references — CLEAN: a36b introduces no orphaned references; it cites `head_sha`/HEAD-binding only to describe the current behavior it wants changed.

comments.author: Joe Oakhart
comments.body: Ownership: the remedy for this over-invalidation bug is owned and IMPLEMENTED by Story 1 'jerky-vista-amok' (22bf), which replaces the claim_gate_check step-3 whole-HEAD exact-match with the content-addressed per-path drift check. This ticket is the failing-case report (repro/expected/actual) and CLOSES when 22bf lands; its prescriptive ACs are the acceptance test for 22bf, not a second implementation. See ADR docs/adr/0002-code-drift-invalidation.md.

compiled_state.description:
## Context

The plan-review claim gate (verify.require_plan_review_for_claim) binds the attestation to the exact code HEAD sha and treats a HEAD mismatch as 'stale', blocking the claim. Because the binding is to the WHOLE commit (not the code the review was actually grounded against), ANY subsequent commit — even one in a completely unrelated file — invalidates a freshly-earned, still-correct attestation.

This is the over-invalidation / false-positive sibling of this epic's under-invalidation concern: 4fcd is about CODE drift NOT being detected (the attestation only binds ticket-material, so relevant code can drift undetected). This bug is the inverse: IRRELEVANT code changes DO invalidate, adding real friction. Both share the root cause — the code-binding is too coarse (whole-HEAD) rather than scoped to the review's resolved edit-set / cited files. The mechanism in this epic's AC1 ('detects material drift in the code ... e.g. the resolved edit-set / cited files') would fix both facets at once.

## Reproduction Steps
1. On a feature branch, run `rebar review-plan <ticketA>` at HEAD=X -> earns a signed attestation bound to X.
2. Commit unrelated work (e.g. another ticket's code, or even a docs typo) -> HEAD advances to Y.
3. Run `rebar claim <ticketA> --assignee me`.

## Expected
The claim succeeds: the attestation is still valid because none of the code ticketA's plan was grounded against changed.

## Actual
The claim is BLOCKED: `Error: cannot claim <ticketA>: attestation is stale (signed at X, HEAD is Y).` The only recovery is to re-run the heavy LLM `review-plan` (minutes + billable) purely because HEAD moved, even though nothing relevant drifted. Observed repeatedly while implementing epic ring-gun-jot: committing sweep N's code staled the already-earned attestation for sweep N+1, forcing a re-review.

## Impact / friction
- Forces serialization (review-plan -> claim must straddle zero intervening commits) and defeats pre-earning attestations in parallel.
- Re-review is slow (~3-5 min) and billable, incurred for no semantic reason.
- Especially painful on multi-ticket branches and shared-checkout test runs that move HEAD.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Claim freshness is scoped to the code the review was grounded against (resolved edit-set / cited files) rather than the whole HEAD sha, so an unrelated commit does NOT stale the attestation.
- [ ] A relevant change (to the cited/edit-set files) STILL invalidates (no regression of this epic's under-invalidation goal).
- [ ] Tested: review at X, commit an unrelated file, claim succeeds; review at X, commit a cited file, claim is blocked.
- [ ] Recovery guidance / friction is reduced (no full re-review required for an unrelated commit).
compiled_state.title: Stale-attestation claim block: ANY new commit (even unrelated) invalidates a fresh plan-review attestation
